student_message: Oh cool! The letter O works perfectly because it is a circle, so every single tiny turn matches exactly. What if we stamp a spiral, does it have rotational symmetry or just look like a dizzy tornado?
final_message: I love your dizzy tornado idea! 🌪️ If you stamp a simple single spiral, like a snail shell, it actually does not have rotational symmetry. When you spin it, the outside tail keeps moving to a new spot, so it never perfectly matches up until you spin it all the way around. But here is a fun secret! If your stamp has a spiral with two or more arms spinning out from the middle, just like a paper pinwheel, it does have rotational symmetry. If you turn a double spiral halfway around, the arms perfectly trade places! So a single tornado spiral is just dizzy, but a pinwheel spiral is wonderfully symmetrical. Can you picture how a pinwheel stamp would look if you turned it? ✨

orchestrator_intent.goal: Explain rotational symmetry in spirals and answer her question.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student is happy, engaged, and asked a specific conceptual question about spirals. The dialogue agent is best suited to explain this.

current_section_content: Block printing is a traditional craft from Rajasthan where artisans carve a design into a wooden block and press it repeatedly onto fabric to create beautiful repeating patterns. When the same block is stamped multiple times in different orientations, the final printed design can have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both. Importantly, the print produced by a block is the mirror image of the carved block, because the block is pressed face-down. By analysing how a block is repeated and rotated, you can predict and identify the symmetry of the resulting pattern.
session_summary: Zara covered reflectional and rotational symmetry in 2D shapes, grids (3x3 and 4x4), and real-world analogies like rubber stamps. She understands these exceptionally well, accurately identifying center pivot points and how alternating colors impact fractional turns. Zara exhibited no struggles. Her learning state remains highly engaged and progressive. She readily applies concepts to new contexts, proactively asking advanced questions about how colors, patterns, and reflections behave in complex designs.
